title: Pierogi z mięsem
subtitle: Polish meat-filled dumplings with pork, mushroom, and caramelised onion.
excerpt: Pierogi z mięsem are Polish dumplings filled with a mix of braised pork and beef mince, dried mushroom, and caramelised onion, a more robust filling than the potato-and-cheese version. Boiled and then fried in butter, they are a complete meal. The filling can use leftover braised meat or raw mince.
sourceNotes: Meat-filled pierogi are a staple of Polish home cooking, particularly associated with Christmas Eve (Wigilia) and Sunday meals. The filling traditionally uses cooked leftover meat, often from a Sunday roast, combined with sautéed mushroom and onion. The porcini mushroom version is most commonly documented in Polish culinary literature of the nineteenth and twentieth centuries.

content.text: Pierogi z mięsem have a more assertive filling than their potato-and-cheese counterpart. The meat is cooked in the pan first, then combined with rehydrated dried mushroom and caramelised onion into a filling with depth and a slightly earthy note. The dried mushroom is not optional. It is what distinguishes this filling from a straightforward mince.

The dough is the same as for pierogi ruskie: flour, egg, butter, water, rested until pliable. The filling can use raw mince cooked through in the pan, or leftover braised meat (shoulder of pork or beef) pulsed in a food processor. The leftover version has more flavour but raw mince is more practical for most cooks.

These are a Christmas Eve dish across Poland, served at the twelve-course Wigilia supper. The Wigilia meal traditionally excludes meat, but pierogi z mięsem are so woven into the tradition that they appear regardless of the filling.

items.name: Dried porcini
items.prepNote: dried porcini, soaked in hot water for 30 minutes, drained and finely chopped

items.name: Onion
items.prepNote: finely chopped, one for the filling, one for serving

content.text: Combine {{plain-flour}} of flour, the {{eggs}} egg, the melted butter, a pinch of salt, and 120 ml of warm water in a large bowl. Mix until a shaggy dough forms, then knead on a lightly floured surface for five minutes until smooth and elastic. Wrap in cling film and rest for thirty minutes.

Melt a knob of butter in a frying pan over medium heat. Add one finely chopped {{onion}} onion and cook for twelve minutes until deep golden. Add the soaked and chopped {{mushrooms-porcini-dried}} porcini and cook for two minutes. Transfer to a bowl. In the same pan over high heat, cook the {{pork-mince}} pork mince and {{beef-mince}} beef mince together, breaking up the meat, for eight minutes until well browned. Season generously with salt and pepper. Combine the mince with the onion and mushroom mixture. Taste and adjust seasoning. Leave to cool completely.

Divide the rested dough into three portions. Roll each portion on a floured surface to about 2 mm thickness. Cut into 9 cm circles. Place a heaped teaspoon of filling in the centre of each circle. Fold into a half-moon and press the edges firmly together, then crimp with your fingers or a fork. Place on a floured tray. Repeat with the remaining dough and filling.
Bring a large pot of generously salted water to the boil. Cook the pierogi in batches of ten: drop in and cook for three to four minutes after they float to the surface. Remove with a slotted spoon.

Melt {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in a wide frying pan over medium-high heat. Fry the boiled pierogi in batches for two minutes on each side until golden and a little crisp at the edges. Caramelise the second onion in butter separately over low heat for twenty minutes until deep golden. Serve the pierogi topped with the caramelised onion and sour cream if using.

items.fix: A heaped teaspoon of filling is the maximum. Press and crimp the edges firmly. Dampen the edge of the dough slightly with water before sealing if the dough has dried out.
items.cause: Overfilled, or edges not sealed tightly.

items.fix: After cooking the mince, tip it into a sieve over a bowl and leave for two minutes to drain before mixing with the onion and mushroom.
items.cause: The mince was not drained of excess fat after cooking.

items.fix: Rest the dough for at least thirty minutes at room temperature. If it tears, gather it back and rest for another ten minutes.

content.text: Meat pierogi are the Christmas pierogi of Poland, served at the Wigilia table on Christmas Eve alongside barszcz and carp. They are also everyday food. Sunday batches get reheated in butter through the week. The porcini in the filling reflects a long Polish tradition of foraging mushrooms in autumn. Dried porcini turn up in many Polish dishes as a flavour base. In Poland the cook would use suszone grzyby, the dried local mushrooms gathered through the year.
